## Winning Direction Summary
- Winning direction: `calm_accountability_direction`.
- Product emotion: grounded first impression, low-friction wake execution, steady recovery after misses.
- Core posture: one primary action per screen in wake-critical moments; progressive disclosure for complexity.
- Monetization stance: free core wake loop remains intact; premium adds reliability tools and deeper insights only.
- No-clone boundary: adapt interaction principles only; do not replicate external visual/copy/motion signatures.

## Global Visual System
- Visual principles: medium-compact density on home/list, low density on active/completion, neutral surfaces, strong text contrast.
- Accent usage: calm blue-green accent reserved for commitment actions and positive confirmations.
- State signaling: hierarchy, text, and iconography first; color never the sole signal.
- Semantic tokens:
  - `color.bg.primary`, `color.bg.secondary`, `color.surface.card`
  - `color.text.primary`, `color.text.secondary`, `color.text.muted`
  - `color.action.primary`, `color.action.primaryPressed`, `color.action.secondary`
  - `color.state.success`, `color.state.warning`, `color.state.destructive`, `color.state.disabled`
  - `color.state.focusRing`, `color.state.highContrastBorder`
- Typography: Dynamic Type semantic styles only, multiline-safe labels, tabular numerals for timers/streak counts.
- Motion: restrained transitions; reduced-motion path uses crossfades/static swaps; no looping urgency animations.

## Global Accessibility Requirements
- VoiceOver: explicit labels and verbs for every status chip, state banner, and primary CTA.
- Focus order: top-down, action-first in wake-critical screens, with fallback action immediately after primary CTA.
- Dynamic Type: no fixed-size text on critical surfaces; multiline expansion with stable card/container growth.
- Contrast: WCAG-safe text/background and chip/border contrast in light, dark, and high-contrast modes.
- Reduced motion: every animated transition has non-motion equivalent with equal semantic clarity.
- Haptic alternatives: all success/warning/failure signals also shown via visible text/icon state.
- Target sizes: interactive targets at least 44x44pt.
- One-handed reach: primary wake CTA within lower-thumb zone on large devices.

## Screens
### onboarding
- Purpose: establish wake intent, default timing, and optional strictness with reversible setup.
- Hierarchy:
  - Step progress and intent headline
  - Current step controls (intent, schedule, strictness)
  - Primary CTA (`Continue`/`Finish setup`)
  - Secondary controls (`Skip for now`, `Back`)
- Primary action: advance setup with explicit confirmation of current choice.
- Secondary actions:
  - `Skip for now` in top-right or bottom secondary button
  - `Back` in nav bar
  - `Learn more` inline disclosure for strictness terms
- Empty state: default recommendation cards (e.g., “Weekday wake at 7:00 AM”) with editable values.
- Loading state: skeleton for step card and CTA; reserved space for helper text to prevent layout jump.
- Error state: inline validation and retry message (e.g., schedule save failed) with preserved form values.
- Copy direction: calm, factual, supportive (“Set your first wake commitment”); avoid guilt or pressure phrases; keep labels short for localization expansion.
- Accessibility requirements:
  - VoiceOver announces step count and current selection.
  - Focus order: title -> step controls -> primary CTA -> secondary actions.
  - Dynamic Type keeps step body multiline without truncating CTA labels.
  - Reduced motion removes step-slide animation.
  - One-handed safe: primary CTA anchored near bottom.
- Screenshot acceptance criteria:
  - Primary CTA visible without scrolling.
  - Step title and CTA not truncated at common Dynamic Type sizes.
  - Skip path visible and clearly secondary.
  - Light/dark screenshots preserve readable hierarchy and non-color state cues.
  - Loading and error variants keep card and CTA positions stable.

### home
- Purpose: show the next wake commitment and provide immediate path to prepare or start.
- Hierarchy:
  - `Next Wake Commitment` module
  - Commitment status line and time context
  - Primary CTA (`Review wake` or `Start when due`)
  - Secondary modules (history preview, profile shortcut)
- Primary action: open the next wake commitment flow.
- Secondary actions:
  - `View all tasks` link below commitment card
  - `History` preview tap target
  - Profile/settings icon in top-right
- Empty state: “No wake commitments yet” with `Create your first wake` CTA.
- Loading state: skeleton commitment card with fixed-height placeholder for status text and CTA.
- Error state: sync/load failure banner with `Retry` and last-known commitment snapshot.
- Copy direction: operational clarity (“Next wake: Tomorrow 7:00 AM”); avoid vague motivation-only copy; localizable date/time formats.
- Accessibility requirements:
  - VoiceOver reads commitment title, due time, and state in one coherent phrase.
  - Focus order prioritizes commitment card and primary CTA before analytics tiles.
  - Dynamic Type wraps status line and keeps CTA fully readable.
  - Contrast-safe status chips with text labels/icons.
  - One-handed reach keeps primary card action in thumb zone.
- Screenshot acceptance criteria:
  - Next commitment card is first visible module.
  - Primary CTA visible without scroll.
  - Status is understandable without relying on color alone.
  - No critical text truncation on common Dynamic Type sizes.
  - Light/dark mode screenshots preserve legibility and hierarchy.

### primary_list
- Purpose: manage all wake tasks grouped by readiness for quick scanning and resume.
- Hierarchy:
  - Readiness sections (`Tonight`, `Later`, `Paused`)
  - Task rows with time, status chip, and quick action
  - Primary list CTA (`Create wake task`)
  - Filters/sort as secondary controls
- Primary action: resume or open the most relevant task in readiness groups.
- Secondary actions:
  - `Pause/Resume` swipe or row action
  - `Edit` from row overflow
  - `Filter` control in header
- Empty state: sectioned guidance with `Create wake task` CTA and short explanation of readiness groups.
- Loading state: section headers and row skeletons with stable row height.
- Error state: inline section error with retry; unaffected sections remain visible.
- Copy direction: plain labels (“Paused”, “Tonight”), no novelty jargon; avoid punitive miss language; ensure short translatable chips.
- Accessibility requirements:
  - VoiceOver announces section + task + state + available action.
  - Focus order follows section header then rows.
  - Dynamic Type allows multiline task titles and wraps metadata.
  - 44x44pt minimum for row actions and overflow menu.
  - Non-haptic cues for pause/resume confirmation.
- Screenshot acceptance criteria:
  - Readiness sections visibly distinct by headings, not color only.
  - At least one row shows clear status chip text/icon.
  - Primary create CTA visible without scroll in empty state.
  - Dynamic Type does not clip row action labels.
  - Loading/error states preserve row geometry.

### create/edit
- Purpose: author or revise a wake task through linear, low-cognitive configuration.
- Hierarchy:
  - Step sequence (`Intent -> Timing -> Safeguards -> Review`)
  - Current step fields
  - Primary CTA (`Continue`/`Save wake task`)
  - Secondary actions (`Cancel`, `Advanced options`)
- Primary action: complete current step and move forward to final save.
- Secondary actions:
  - `Advanced options` disclosure below core fields
  - `Cancel` in nav bar
  - `Back` to previous step
- Empty state: prefilled starter values with concise explanations and defaults.
- Loading state: form section skeletons with preserved field spacing and fixed footer CTA area.
- Error state: inline field errors and top summary for save/sync failure with retry.
- Copy direction: explicit and instructional (“Choose your wake window”); avoid legalistic or alarmist phrasing; localize numeric/time units cleanly.
- Accessibility requirements:
  - VoiceOver reads field label, value, and validation state.
  - Focus order is step header -> fields -> primary CTA -> secondary actions.
  - Dynamic Type allows multiline helper text and non-overlapping controls.
  - Reduced motion disables animated step transitions.
  - One-handed reach keeps primary CTA in bottom action bar.
- Screenshot acceptance criteria:
  - Current step and progress context clearly visible.
  - Primary CTA visible without scrolling on each step.
  - Error screenshot shows preserved input and clear correction guidance.
  - No truncation of key field labels at common Dynamic Type sizes.
  - Layout stability between loading and filled states.

### active_task
- Purpose: execute a live wake commitment with one dominant action and explicit state.
- Hierarchy:
  - Current-state banner (e.g., `Wake Check In Progress`)
  - Dominant primary CTA (`I'm awake` or equivalent state action)
  - Fallback action (clearly labeled, lower emphasis)
  - Minimal contextual details/timer
- Primary action: confirm wake completion step for current state.
- Secondary actions:
  - `Need extra minute` fallback button below primary CTA
  - `View details` collapsed disclosure
- Empty state: not applicable; if no active task, redirect to home with explanatory message.
- Loading state: state banner and disabled CTA placeholders while status sync resolves.
- Error state: explicit sync/permission failure banner with retry and safe fallback path.
- Copy direction: short imperative clarity (“Confirm you’re awake”); avoid shaming, urgency theatrics, or dense text; localization-safe verbs.
- Accessibility requirements:
  - VoiceOver announces current state before actionable controls.
  - Focus order: state banner -> primary CTA -> fallback -> details.
  - High contrast for primary action and state text in both themes.
  - Reduced motion removes transition flourishes; state updates remain textual.
  - Primary CTA 44x44pt minimum and thumb reachable.
- Screenshot acceptance criteria:
  - Exactly one visually dominant primary action.
  - Fallback action is visible and clearly secondary.
  - State is understandable without motion or color-only cues.
  - Dark/light screenshots both show clear CTA contrast.
  - Loading/error variants keep CTA and banner positions stable.

### completion
- Purpose: confirm outcome, reinforce continuity, and route to one next-best action.
- Hierarchy:
  - Completion confirmation message
  - Outcome summary (time/status)
  - Primary next action (`Set next wake` or `Quick reflection`)
  - Secondary repair/help actions
- Primary action: choose the recommended next step to sustain continuity.
- Secondary actions:
  - `View history` link
  - `Repair missed wake` when relevant
  - Dismiss/back to home
- Empty state: if opened without prior task, show neutral guidance and `Go to home` CTA.
- Loading state: confirmation card skeleton with reserved next-action button space.
- Error state: failure saving completion/notes shows retry with preserved summary.
- Copy direction: respectful, factual (“Wake completed at 7:02 AM”); avoid celebratory pressure or guilt; keep concise localizable phrasing.
- Accessibility requirements:
  - VoiceOver announces completion state and next action recommendation.
  - Focus order prioritizes next action before tertiary links.
  - Dynamic Type keeps summary and CTA multiline-safe.
  - Non-color indicators for success/recovery states.
  - Haptic confirmation always paired with text/icon confirmation.
- Screenshot acceptance criteria:
  - Confirmation state is immediately visible above fold.
  - Primary next action visible without scroll.
  - Recovery path present for missed/partial outcomes without punitive copy.
  - No key truncation in common Dynamic Type sizes.
  - Visual structure stable across loading/error states.

### history/streaks
- Purpose: show reliability trend, explain outcome reasons, and enable repair planning.
- Hierarchy:
  - Reliability summary/trend module
  - Reason-coded entries (`completed`, `missed`, `recovered`)
  - Primary CTA (`Plan repair` when needed or `Set next wake`)
  - Secondary filters/time-range controls
- Primary action: initiate repair planning or next wake setup from current reliability context.
- Secondary actions:
  - Time-range segmented control
  - Entry detail drill-in
  - Export/share if available as tertiary
- Empty state: no history yet message with `Complete your first wake` guidance.
- Loading state: chart/list skeletons with fixed axes/header footprint to avoid jump.
- Error state: partial data notice with retry; stale cached entries remain visible.
- Copy direction: explanatory accountability (“Missed: alarm silenced early”); avoid blame language; keep reason labels short and translatable.
- Accessibility requirements:
  - VoiceOver reads trend direction and each entry reason as text.
  - Focus order: summary -> primary CTA -> entries -> filters.
  - Dynamic Type supports multiline reason text and chip expansion.
  - Charts/rings must have text equivalents.
  - One-handed reach for primary repair CTA.
- Screenshot acceptance criteria:
  - Reason-coded outcomes visible as text, not color alone.
  - Primary action visible without scroll in relevant state.
  - Trend remains legible in light/dark modes.
  - Dynamic Type does not clip reason labels.
  - Loading/error maintain stable chart/list containers.

### profile/settings
- Purpose: separate mission controls from preferences, billing, and legal/support.
- Hierarchy:
  - Mission Controls section
  - Preferences section
  - Account/Billing section
  - Help/Legal section
- Primary action: access mission-critical settings quickly and safely.
- Secondary actions:
  - Manage subscription within Account/Billing
  - Help/contact actions in Help/Legal
  - Sign out/delete account as guarded destructive flows
- Empty state: account-not-synced state with sign-in/retry prompt and local settings access.
- Loading state: section skeleton rows with fixed grouping spacing.
- Error state: account/billing fetch error inline with retry; mission settings remain accessible.
- Copy direction: plain and specific section labels; avoid mixing legal/account terms with wake execution labels; localization-safe row titles.
- Accessibility requirements:
  - VoiceOver announces section boundaries and row purpose.
  - Focus order follows section order, mission-first.
  - Dynamic Type allows multiline row labels and subtitles.
  - Destructive actions include explicit text/icon confirmation.
  - Touch targets for rows and toggles meet minimum size.
- Screenshot acceptance criteria:
  - Section separation is visually obvious.
  - Mission Controls appears above Account/Billing and Help/Legal.
  - No truncation of section headers or row labels at common Dynamic Type sizes.

        case .destructive: WakeColorTokens.destructive

public struct WakeCard<Content: View>: View {
    public enum Style {
        case standard
        case elevated
        case tinted
    }

    private let style: Style
    private let content: Content
    @Environment(\.colorScheme) private var colorScheme

    public init(
        style: Style = .standard,
        @ViewBuilder content: () -> Content
    ) {
        self.style = style
        self.content = content()
    }

    public var body: some View {
        content
            .padding(WakeSpacing.md)
            .frame(maxWidth: .infinity, alignment: .leading)
            .background(background)
            .clipShape(RoundedRectangle(cornerRadius: DSRadius.md, style: .continuous))
            .overlay(
                RoundedRectangle(cornerRadius: DSRadius.md, style: .continuous)
                    .stroke(WakeColorTokens.highContrastBorder.opacity(0.2), lineWidth: 1)
            )
            .shadow(
                color: colorScheme == .dark ? WakeColorTokens.darkModeCardShadow : WakeColorTokens.lightModeCardShadow,
                radius: style == .elevated ? 12 : 0,
                x: 0,
                y: style == .elevated ? 6 : 0
            )
    }

    private var background: Color {
        switch style {
        case .standard: WakeColorTokens.surfaceCard
        case .elevated: WakeColorTokens.surfaceCard
        case .tinted: WakeColorTokens.actionSecondary
        }
    }
}

public struct WakeRow: View {
    private let title: String
    private let subtitle: String?
    private let chipText: String?
    private let chipTone: WakeStateTone
    private let icon: String?

    public init(
        title: String,
        subtitle: String? = nil,
        chipText: String? = nil,
        chipTone: WakeStateTone = .active,
        icon: String? = nil
    ) {
        self.title = title
        self.subtitle = subtitle
        self.chipText = chipText
        self.chipTone = chipTone
        self.icon = icon
    }

    public var body: some View {
        HStack(alignment: .top, spacing: WakeSpacing.sm) {
            if let icon {
                Image(systemName: icon)
                    .foregroundStyle(WakeColorTokens.textSecondary)
                    .accessibilityHidden(true)
            }

            VStack(alignment: .leading, spacing: WakeSpacing.xxs) {
                Text(title)
                    .wakeType(WakeTypography.bodyStrong, lineLimit: WakeTypography.lineLimit(for: .body))
                    .foregroundStyle(WakeColorTokens.textPrimary)
                if let subtitle {
                    Text(subtitle)
                        .wakeType(WakeTypography.detail, lineLimit: WakeTypography.lineLimit(for: .subheadline))
                        .foregroundStyle(WakeColorTokens.textSecondary)
                }
            }
            Spacer(minLength: WakeSpacing.xs)
            if let chipText {
                WakeStatusChip(text: chipText, tone: chipTone)
            }
        }
        .frame(minHeight: 44)
        .accessibilityElement(children: .combine)
        .accessibilityLabel(Text(accessibilitySummary))
        .wakeMinimumTapTarget()
    }

    private var accessibilitySummary: String {
        var segments = [title]
        if let subtitle { segments.append(subtitle) }
        if let chipText { segments.append(chipText) }
        return segments.joined(separator: ", ")
    }
}

public struct WakeStatusChip: View {
    let text: String
    let tone: WakeStateTone

    public init(text: String, tone: WakeStateTone) {
        self.text = text
        self.tone = tone
    }

    public var body: some View {
        HStack(spacing: WakeSpacing.xxs) {
            Image(systemName: tone.iconName)
                .font(.caption2)
                .accessibilityHidden(true)
            Text(text)
                .font(WakeTypography.status)
                .lineLimit(2)
        }
        .foregroundStyle(tone.color)
        .padding(.horizontal, WakeSpacing.xs)
        .padding(.vertical, WakeSpacing.xxs)
        .background(tone.color.opacity(0.13))
        .clipShape(Capsule())
        .overlay(
            Capsule()
                .stroke(WakeColorTokens.highContrastBorder.opacity(0.25), lineWidth: 1)
        )
        .accessibilityElement(children: .ignore)
        .accessibilityLabel(Text(text))
        .accessibilityAddTraits(.isStaticText)
    }
}

public struct WakeProgressStatus: View {
    private let title: String
    private let message: String
    private let progress: Double
    private let tone: WakeStateTone

    public init(title: String, message: String, progress: Double, tone: WakeStateTone = .active) {
        self.title = title
        self.message = message
        self.progress = progress
        self.tone = tone
    }

    public var body: some View {
        VStack(alignment: .leading, spacing: WakeSpacing.xs) {
            Text(title)
                .wakeType(WakeTypography.title, lineLimit: WakeTypography.lineLimit(for: .title3))
            Text(message)
                .wakeType(WakeTypography.detail, lineLimit: WakeTypography.lineLimit(for: .subheadline))
                .foregroundStyle(WakeColorTokens.textSecondary)
            ProgressView(value: min(max(progress, 0), 1))
                .tint(tone.color)
                .accessibilityValue("\(Int(progress * 100))%")
        }
        .accessibilityElement(children: .combine)
        .wakeStateAccessibility(label: title, hint: message)
    }
}

public struct WakePrimaryActionButton: View {
    private let title: LocalizedStringKey
    private let role: ButtonRole?
    private let action: () -> Void
    @Environment(\.accessibilityReduceMotion) private var reduceMotion

    public init(_ title: LocalizedStringKey, role: ButtonRole? = nil, action: @escaping () -> Void) {
        self.title = title
        self.role = role

        self.title = LocalizedStringKey(title)
        self.role = role
        self.action = action
    }

    public var body: some View {
        Button(role: role) {
            Haptics.tap()
            action()
        } label: {
            Text(title)
                .wakeType(WakeTypography.bodyStrong, lineLimit: WakeTypography.lineLimit(for: .body))
                .frame(maxWidth: .infinity)
                .frame(minHeight: 44)
        }
        .buttonStyle(.borderedProminent)
        .tint(role == .destructive ? WakeColorTokens.destructive : WakeColorTokens.actionPrimary)
        .scaleEffect(reduceMotion ? 1 : 0.999)
        .accessibilityHint(Text("Activates the primary action"))
        .accessibilityAddTraits(.isButton)

    }
}

# UX Implement Visual System

Implement only the visual system portion of the UX Studio direction in `apps/waketask-ios`.

## Scope

Implement or update:

- design tokens and semantic theme colors for light mode, dark mode, high contrast, disabled, destructive, success, warning, premium, and active task states

.workflow/iphone-app-ux-studio/design/cross-critique.md:21:- `hard_wake_direction`: Premium promise around serious reliability tools is clear and differentiated for high-intent users. Risk: hard tone can reduce affinity and trial conversion for users who want support rather than pressure.

.workflow/iphone-app-ux-studio/design/directions/minimal_native_direction.md:4:The first 10 seconds should feel immediate, legible, and controlled: the user can tell what alarm/task is active without parsing decoration. In the wake state, the app should feel firm but not chaotic, with one dominant action and status feedback that can be understood half-asleep. After task completion, the emotion should shift to quiet relief and confidence rather than celebration overload.
.workflow/iphone-app-ux-studio/design/directions/minimal_native_direction.md:7:Use native iOS structure, restrained surfaces, high contrast, and familiar controls. Layout density should stay low in active wake flows and moderately dense in planning/history screens. Component shape language should use standard rounded rectangles, lists, segmented controls, sheets, and compact status rows rather than bespoke game UI. Hierarchy should prioritize next alarm, required task, progress, and failure recovery.
.workflow/iphone-app-ux-studio/design/directions/minimal_native_direction.md:13:Use Dynamic Type-first roles: large title for alarm time or active-task state, headline for task names, body for instructions, caption for secondary metadata. Numeric treatment should use tabular digits for times, countdowns, streaks, and step counts. Minimum readable sizes should respect iOS body defaults; no micro labels in the wake path.
.workflow/iphone-app-ux-studio/design/directions/minimal_native_direction.md:30:Premium can promise customization, richer challenge packs, analytics, and backup reliability. Free should include a complete alarm/task flow. Avoid coercive paywalls in the active wake state, streak hostage mechanics, or blocking alarm dismissal behind payment.
.workflow/iphone-app-ux-studio/design/directions/minimal_native_direction.md:36:This direction has low SwiftUI risk because it uses native components and semantic tokens. Risks are mostly in making active-task states visually distinct without custom complexity and ensuring screenshots/Appium flows can verify all states. It avoids broad boilerplate rewrites.
